statement: Iceland participates in the EU Emissions Trading System (EU ETS) through a bilateral agreement with the EU (Decision 2015/1814 and predecessors), not through the EEA Agreement. The arrangement covers Iceland's industrial emissions, primarily from aluminium smelting and ferrosilicon production. In 2023, Iceland's EU ETS-covered installations accounted for roughly 1.8 million tonnes CO₂-equivalent, with the three aluminium smelters as the dominant participants. EU ETS allowance prices rose from under 10 EUR/tonne in 2018 to approximately 60–80 EUR/tonne in 2023–2024, significantly increasing compliance costs for Icelandic industry.
source_name: Umhverfisstofnun — Iceland's participation in EU ETS; European Commission — EU ETS data
caveats: Iceland's EU ETS participation terms have been negotiated bilaterally and include some transitional provisions. Full EU membership would bring Iceland fully into the ETS framework without bilateral negotiation. The ETS is being reformed under 'Fit for 55' — ETS2 will extend carbon pricing to buildings and road transport from 2027, which would affect Iceland differently given its renewable heating.

related_entries.statement: Through the EEA Agreement, Iceland has already incorporated a substantial body of EU energy legislation, including the Third Energy Package (Directive 2009/72/EC on electricity and 2009/73/EC on gas), the Renewable Energy Directive (2009/28/EC and its 2018 recast), and energy efficiency directives. However, Iceland secured key adaptations: it is exempt from the EU's gas market rules (having no gas infrastructure) and has adaptations recognising its unique energy mix. The EEA Joint Committee Decision No 93/2017 incorporated the Third Energy Package with Icelandic adaptations. Iceland has not incorporated the EU Emissions Trading System into the EEA Agreement, instead maintaining a separate arrangement.

Iceland has committed under the Paris Agreement to a 55% reduction in greenhouse gas emissions by 2030 compared to 1990 levels, aligned with the EU's target through the EEA joint fulfilment arrangement. Iceland's Climate Action Plan (Aðgerðaáætlun í loftslagsmálum, 2020, updated 2024) outlines sectoral measures. However, Iceland's emissions profile is unusual: industrial process emissions from aluminium smelting are large, agriculture (sheep farming, dairy) contributes significantly, and LULUCF is a major source due to degraded wetlands covering roughly 10% of the country. Total emissions excluding LULUCF were approximately 3.5 Mt CO₂-eq in 2023, while LULUCF added roughly 8–10 Mt CO₂-eq, making Iceland a substantial net emitter relative to its population of 390,000.

citations.report_title: „Við hefðum náð meiri árangri með því að vera innan Evrópusambandsins“

claims.claim_text: Íslensk stjórnvöld hefðu náð betri árangri í samningaviðræðum um undanþágur frá ETS-kerfinu ef Ísland hefði verið aðili að ESB.
Losun alþjóðaflugs á gróðurhúsalofttegundum innan ESB fellur undir ETS-viðskiptakerfið.
Ísland á aðild að ETS-viðskiptakerfinu í gegnum EES-samninginn.
Ísland hefur sérstaka undanþágu frá losunarheimildum í ETS-kerfinu sem rennur út í lok árs.
Til stendur að framlengja undanþágu Íslands frá ETS-kerfinu út árið 2027.
